text: no way I was going anywhere with him. But I played along for a bit. I said I’d think about it. I kept going to our sessions for a couple more weeks, hoping he might let slip where the cave was. But he never did. In the end I stopped going’ ‘I don’t suppose you ever found out where the recording was made?’ ‘No, but I found a guy on a forum who specialises in record- ing water and I sent him the recording. Just on the off chance. He listened to it and he said something really strange. He didn’t think it was a cave at all. He thought that that booming sound I’d picked out meant it couldn’t be a cave. He said it had to be a building. A flooded cathedral was his guess. We did some research to try to find possible locations, but we didn’t get anywhere.’ Silence. ‘And Dr Ketterley didn’t say anything about a maze? A maze in a deserted garden?’ ‘Umm ... No. I don’t think so.’ ‘And he never mentioned a man called Matthew Rose Sorensen?’ ‘No.’ “You’re sure?’ “Yes. oa? = ol 2 7 = * ihanasbe a whe sti meee -- ae Pit = : wie elo re r mee: » igh oe * ae _= 2 f - 4 t t q 7 » oe i + Ao J a 4 . . 4 > ™ peer) Neel bow UNE @ R Susanna Clarke is the author of Jonathan Strange & Mr Norrell and The Ladies of Grace Adieu and Other Stories. This typeface is an adaptation of a style of letter that had been popularised for monumental work in stone by Eric Gill. Large scale drawings by Gill were given to Charles Malin, a Parisian punch-cutter, and his hand-cut punches were the basis for the font issued by Monotype. First used in a private translation called “The Passion of Perpetua and Felicity’, the italic was originally called Felicity. — bal) oY gi Le 1 ens te ent ; ee! S a olen 4 das _ & snk oo Rene al : 1 gS eld 9d y “~ = | iKeenl bss tralle 3" Al NN rth id ‘ z ae th a a at ty 1 Susanna Clarke’s debut novel, Jonathan Strange & Mr Norrell, was longlisted for the Man Booker Prize and shortlisted for the Whitbread First Novel Award and the Guardian First Book Award. It won the British Book Awards Newcomer of the Year, the Hugo Award and the World Fantasy Award in 2005. Susanna Clarke is also the author of the short story collection The Ladies of Grace Adieu. She lives in Derbyshire. Piranesiis an exquisite puzzle-box’ DAVID MITCHELL ‘A gorgeous, spellbinding mystery ... A treasure, washed up upon a forgotten shore, waiting to be discovered’ ERIN MORGENSTERN
